title: Ankara Braces for NATO Summit as Erdogan Leverages Trump Alliance
subhead: Turkey enters a pivotal week: hosting the NATO summit July 7-8, advancing a $700M jet engine deal with Washington, cracking down on dissent and LGBTQ visibility at home, and positioning itself as a regional mediator on the Iran deal.

sections.heading: NATO Summit in Ankara: Stakes, Optics, and Crackdowns
sections.body:
The NATO summit opens in Ankara on July 7-8 with a formal Defense Industry Forum kicking off at 10:00 a.m. local time on July 7, followed by a NATO-Ukraine Council meeting at the foreign minister level and a dinner for heads of state hosted by President Erdogan. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy will attend the dinner and deliver a joint statement with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, according to the official program released by NATO and reported by Kyiv Post.

The summit comes at a fraught moment for the alliance. European officials are pushing to demonstrate increased defense spending — Rutte said NATO's European members and Canada spent $570 billion in 2025, up $90 billion from the previous year — while trying to smooth over transatlantic tensions stemming from the US-Iran war and Trump's threats to reduce American commitments in Europe. Trump complained on Truth Social last week that the US was spending money to protect NATO members 'without getting any benefit,' according to Cyprus Mail.

Turkish authorities have rolled out extensive security measures: over 200 arrests, road closures, business shutdowns, and the cancellation of dozens of public events. The Turkish Presidential Office has also implemented what Kyiv Post described as a 'stringent vetting process' for journalist accreditation, excluding several opposition media outlets. Former Ambassador Namık Tan publicly criticized the restrictions, noting such extensive domestic lockdowns have 'no precedent in the alliance's history.'
sections.whyItMatters: Ankara is using the summit to showcase Turkey's centrality to NATO at a time when European allies are desperate to keep Trump engaged. But the sweeping security crackdown and media exclusions underscore the domestic cost of Erdogan's strategy.

sections.heading: The Trump-Erdogan Bonanza: F-110 Engines and the F-35 Question
sections.body:
President Trump confirmed he will attend the Ankara summit, telling reporters: 'I would not have gone for most people... I'm going out of respect to President Erdogan.' Trump hinted he would make news during the visit related to jet engines and potential F-35 sales, saying: 'I'm going to probably do something that's going to make him very happy,' according to CNBC.

The centerpiece is the proposed $700 million sale of F110-GE-129E/F engines for Turkey's domestically produced KAAN fifth-generation fighter jet. The State Department took a significant step last week by notifying key lawmakers of its plan to bypass congressional opposition to the sale, CNBC reported. However, the deal faces resistance. Rep. Gregory Meeks, top Democrat on the House Foreign Affairs Committee, criticized the administration for not providing a written justification, and Rep. Dina Titus (D-NV) introduced a Joint Resolution of Disapproval on July 3 to block the sale, Defense Express reported.

On the F-35 front, Turkey was expelled from the program in 2019 over its purchase of Russian S-400 missile defense systems. VP JD Vance said Washington was 'exploring ways' to sell the jets, but significant bipartisan opposition remains — most notably from Sen. Jim Risch (R-ID), chairman of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, who opposes any F-35 sale while Turkey retains the S-400s, according to CNBC.

The broader thaw is also visible in the Justice Department's earlier decision to drop the Halkbank sanctions-evasion case, removing a major irritant in bilateral relations, CNBC reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The engine sale is a test case for how far Trump's personal rapport with Erdogan can override institutional resistance in Washington. If the F-110 deal goes through, it keeps KAAN viable; if not, Turkey's fifth-gen fighter program faces years of delay.

sections.heading: Erdogan Positions Turkey as Regional Mediator on Iran Deal
sections.body:
Standing alongside Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif in Istanbul on July 4, Erdogan warned that Israel must not be allowed to 'dynamite' the US-Iran peace deal, Reuters reported. 'No solution that does not take strength from the will and contributions of regional countries can be lasting,' Erdogan said, praising what he called the Islamabad Memorandum — the interim understanding between the US and Iran mediated by Pakistan.

Erdogan's remarks reflect a dual strategy: criticizing Israel's military operations in Gaza, Lebanon, and Syria while positioning Turkey as an indispensable regional voice in the peace process. Turkey has repeatedly accused Israel of trying to undermine the US-Iran deal, though the claim remains a political assertion by Ankara and has not been independently verified.

On the bilateral front with Pakistan, Erdogan said Turkey aimed to deepen cooperation in energy, transport, critical minerals, IT, and defense, with a $5 billion trade target. Turkish Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar said Turkish firms want to contribute to Pakistan's electricity sector transformation, according to Reuters.
sections.whyItMatters: Erdogan is using the Iran deal's fragility to reassert Turkey's relevance as a regional mediator — a role he has consistently sought across conflicts from Ukraine to Gaza. The Pakistan alignment also deepens Turkey's outreach to non-Western partners.

sections.heading: Domestic Crackdown: Comedian Jailed, LGBTQ Cruise Banned, Press Restricted
sections.body:
A Turkish court on July 3 ordered comedian Deniz Göktaş jailed pending trial on charges of insulting religious values and President Erdogan, according to Anadolu Agency. Göktaş was detained at Istanbul's main airport on his return from abroad, days after prosecutors launched an investigation into his stand-up routine, which drew 9.5 million YouTube views after being uploaded on June 24. During questioning, Göktaş said his remarks — including calling Erdogan a 'dictator' — were satirical and reflected a topic widely debated in Turkey. Insulting the president carries a sentence of up to four years in prison.

Separately, Turkish authorities barred a Virgin Voyages cruise ship chartered by LGBTQ travel company Atlantis Events from docking at Kuşadası on July 7 or Istanbul, citing 'moral values.' Authorities in Aydın province said the group was 'known for behaviors incompatible with the fabric of our society and our moral values.' Istanbul police also raided a bar allegedly because an 'Atlantis brochure' promoted a party there, according to Metro Weekly. The ship, carrying approximately 1,900 passengers, was rerouted to Cairo and Crete. It is the first time in Atlantis Events' 36-year history that a port has actively refused entry based on the identity of passengers, CEO Rich Campbell told CNN.

These developments come alongside the NATO summit media accreditation restrictions that excluded opposition outlets — a pattern of shrinking civic space that is intensifying in the lead-up to the summit.
sections.whyItMatters: The Göktaş detention and the cruise ban are not isolated incidents — they are part of a sustained effort to demonstrate ideological red lines ahead of a moment where global attention is on Ankara. The timing, days before the NATO summit, signals that Erdogan sees no political cost to escalating domestic social conservatism while courting Western leaders.

sections.heading: Pension Hikes and Inflation Fatigue: June Data Triggers Public Pushback
sections.body:
Turkey's statistical institute TÜİK released June inflation figures this week, triggering the standard six-month pension and civil servant salary adjustments. While specific inflation rates were not available in English-language sources, Turkish media coverage indicates widespread dissatisfaction with the resulting increases. The union confederation KESK staged protests in Sinop demanding emergency supplementary raises, criticizing TÜİK's data and redirecting anger at the cost of hosting the NATO summit, according to local reports.

Multiple outlets reported that the lowest pension bracket applies to approximately 5 million retirees, costing the budget 79 billion lira — a figure that underscores the strain on public finances. Retirees across several provinces voiced frustration that the increases do not cover rising rents and basic needs, according to local news sites including Urfanın Nabzı and Malatya Güncel.
sections.whyItMatters: The disconnect between NATO summit spectacle and pensioner frustration is a recurring political fault line in Turkey. With elections on the horizon, the AKP's ability to manage inflation expectations while spending on summit optics will be tested.

sections.heading: Putin-Trump Call Previews Summit Geopolitics
sections.body:
Russian President Vladimir Putin and US President Donald Trump held a nearly 90-minute phone call on July 4 in which they discussed the Ukraine war and the upcoming NATO summit in Ankara, according to multiple reports. Zelenskiy also spoke with Trump, calling for 'American resolve' to help end the war and telling Trump there is 'a real prospect to end this war,' The Guardian reported.

The call comes as Al-Monitor reported that Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan will travel to Moscow to reiterate Ankara's offer to host Russia-Ukraine peace negotiations. Turkey's positioning as a potential peace broker remains a key diplomatic asset, though the actual prospects for negotiations remain unclear.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is simultaneously hosting the NATO summit where Ukraine will be a central agenda item and courting Moscow as a potential peace mediator. The dual track gives Erdogan leverage with both sides — but only if the Trump-Putin dynamic doesn't marginalize Ankara's role.

bottomLine: Turkey enters a defining week: it hosts the NATO summit July 7-8 as Erdogan leverages his personal rapport with Trump to unlock defense deals worth hundreds of millions of dollars, while simultaneously cracking down on dissent and LGBTQ visibility at home. The $700 million F-110 engine sale for the KAAN fighter hangs in the balance between Trump's executive authority and congressional resistance. Regionally, Erdogan is positioning Turkey as essential to the Iran deal process and as a potential Ukraine peace broker — all while pensioner anger over inflation simmers at home. The summit will reveal whether Erdogan's bet on Trump translates into durable strategic gains or merely transactional optics.
sources: CNBC, Reuters, Kyiv Post, Defense Express, New York Post, Metro Weekly, AsiaOne, Anadolu Agency, Cyprus Mail, Jang News, Al-Monitor, Khaama Press, The Guardian, T24, Takvim, Türkiye Gazetesi, Sondakika, Habertürk
